title: Chapter 26: The Double Game
content: Heath Cruz was magnificent.

I watched from the second-floor gallery as he strode through the atrium, the human equivalent of a supervoltage line dropped into standing water. Students parted like a shoal of fish catching the shadow of a hawk. Faculty pressed themselves against the wainscoting. Even the receptionist — forty years at Blackwood, impervious to every headmaster and chairman who'd crossed her desk — flinched when he passed.

Three faculty terminations in two weeks. The entire arts enrichment budget slashed to zero. A demand for itemized expense reports from every department head going back twelve years, with the subtle implication that refusal would be treated as an admission of malfeasance.
He was a butcher. A tyrant. A man who'd walked into a school that ran on tradition and whispered agreements and started swinging a meat cleaver.
He was exactly what I'd designed.

I pressed my palm flat against the cool balustrade and let myself feel it — the bright, dangerous pleasure of watching my architecture hold. Every move Heath made in public was one we'd mapped in his office at midnight, heads bent together over my father's org chart, my handwriting next to his in the margins. He'd argued against the arts cut. *Too visible. Too fast.* I'd overridden him. *That's the point. You need to be the story. As long as the school is talking about you, no one's looking at what you're actually after.*

The story wrote itself. Chairman Cruz, the outsider who didn't understand Blackwood's culture. The brute with the balance sheet. Students organized a sit-in that the local paper covered. Alumni threatened to pull donations. Parents called the administration in droves, voices shaking with the particular panic of people who'd paid seven figures for their children's belonging and now watched the infrastructure of that belonging get dismantled.
None of them looked at the audit.

None of them noticed the quiet documents moving through the business office — requests for financial records that the board had buried for years, now resurfacing in Heath's inbox because he'd made them impossible to ignore.
He was the blade. I was the hand.
And the hand was having the time of its life.
The dead drop system was my idea. Heath had wanted encrypted messaging — secure, efficient, soulless. I'd wanted something that required us to think like each other.

The library was the primary artery. Third floor, east wing, row 17-K: the section on nineteenth-century educational philosophy that no student had touched since the building was renovated. I'd pull a book — *The Idea of a University* was our favorite — and leave marginalia in pencil. A question mark next to a paragraph about institutional trust meant *new financial irregularity found, check the cloud folder.* An underlined passage about discipline meant *Robert is asking questions, we need to meet.*

Heath's responses came in the piano sheet music he left on the Steinway in the music room after hours. I knew enough about notation to decode his annotations — a crescendo marking above a measure that should have been pianissimo meant *escalate,* a fermata over a rest meant *hold position.* The first time I'd deciphered one of his messages, sitting alone at the piano at eleven at night with the lid half-closed and my breath held, something in my chest had lit up like a struck match. The intimacy of it. The conspiracy written in a language only we could read.

The cloud folder was the backstop — a shared drive disguised as a school archive, buried under three layers of administrative subfolders. We never accessed it from our own devices. Library computers, always. I'd upload photographs of board meeting minutes I'd taken with my phone during social events, angle obscured by my champagne flute. Audio clips from faculty grievance sessions I'd recorded in the garden when I was supposed to be smoking. Donor conversations I'd memorized and transcribed later, every name and number and nervous laugh preserved in the archive like insects in amber.
Every drop was a delivery. Every coded message was a letter pressed into the skin of someone who'd know exactly how to read it.
The spy game was the most intimate thing I'd ever done with another human being.

The board reception was held in the north parlor, a room that smelled of old wood and older money — the particular bergamot-and-leather scent that clung to rooms where important men had made unimportant decisions for a century. French doors opened onto the garden. A string quartet played something forgettable. Waiters moved through the crowd with silver trays, and the champagne was the good stuff, the kind that tasted like citrus and limestone and the specific self-satisfaction of people who could afford to drink it on a Wednesday.
I stood near the fireplace, champagne in hand, watching Heath cross the room toward my father.

Robert was holding court with two board members — the Whitmore woman and the older Ashworth, the one whose shell companies I'd been mapping for three weeks. He saw Heath coming. His expression didn't change, but his hand moved to the lapel of his jacket, a self-soothing gesture I'd catalogued in childhood. Robert Vance adjusting his armor.
„Robert.“ Heath's voice carried just enough to turn nearby heads. That was deliberate, too. „I wanted to catch you before the formal session next week.“
The Whitmore woman and Ashworth shifted, uncomfortable witnesses to a confrontation they couldn't leave without making it obvious.
„Of course.“ My father's smile was the one he used on people he considered beneath him — warm on the surface, sclerotic underneath. „What can I do for you, Chairman?“

„Your financial oversight has been...“ Heath paused, sipped his own champagne, let the silence stretch until the Whitmore woman's knuckles whitened around her glass. „Creative. I look forward to our audit bringing some clarity.“
The word *creative* hung in the air like smoke.

Robert's face became stone. Not angry — stone was worse than anger. Stone meant he was calculating, filing the threat away, weighing responses. „I'm sure the board will welcome the transparency,“ he said, and the lie was so smooth it almost sounded like the truth.
Heath nodded once and moved on, leaving Robert standing among the ruins of his composure.

I lifted my champagne to my lips. The bubbles traced a bright cold line down my throat. Across the room, my eyes met Heath's for exactly one second — a flicker, nothing more, the kind of glance that could mean anything or nothing.
Inside, I was incandescent.

I'd placed the bait. I'd chosen the moment. I'd told Heath which board members to pressure, which financial thread to pull, which words would land like shrapnel inside my father's carefully maintained facade. And now I was watching it work, standing six feet away with champagne on my tongue and my face arranged into the precise expression of a daughter who was as startled as everyone else.
No one looked at me. Why would they? I was Robert Vance's daughter. The fiancée of Julian Ashworth. A decorative element in a room full of men who mattered.
I'd never been more dangerous than I was in that moment.

His office door had a deadlock that the previous chairman had never bothered to change. I heard the bolt turn — the solid, satisfying thunk of metal engaging — and felt my shoulders drop for the first time all evening.
The performance was over.

Heath turned from the door. The chairman's mask came off like a second skin peeling away, and underneath was the man I'd been writing secret letters to in the margins of Jesuit philosophy. His jaw unclenched. His eyes lost their broadcast-ready flatness. He leaned against the door and exhaled — a long, slow release that seemed to drain ten years of forced authority from his frame.
„He took it well,“ he said.

„He took it exactly how I told you he would.“ I set my champagne glass on his desk and reached into my purse. The flash drive was small and cold in my hand, its metal casing warmed slightly by the heat of my palm. I felt the USB edges press into my fingers as I held it out. „Everything on the Ashworth shell companies. His father's, the two in Delaware, the one in the Caymans that routes through a law firm in Geneva. Sloane's father was the tip — the whole board is dirty.“
Heath took it. Our fingers touched.
One beat. Two.

His fingertips were rough — a detail I'd catalogued weeks ago and couldn't stop returning to, the calluses from old guitar habits or construction work or whatever life he'd lived before he became this man in this room. They grazed the inside of my knuckle, and the contact sent a current up my arm that I absolutely refused to acknowledge.
He pulled the drive away. I curled my hand into a fist at my side, the ghost of his touch still printed on my skin like a brand.
„The Geneva routing is new,“ he said, his voice carefully even.
„Elena Ashworth's garden party. Her husband drinks Campari and talks too much. I pretended to be interested in his skiing holiday.“ I paused. „I was very convincing.“
Something flickered in Heath's expression — not jealousy, not quite. Something adjacent to it. Something that lived in the same neighborhood but kept different hours. „I don't doubt it.“

He moved to his desk and plugged the drive into his laptop. The screen's glow turned his face to blue-white architecture in the dark room, and I watched the data populate — file after file, dates and sums and corporate names that spelled out decades of systematic theft from the school's endowment.
„This is enough,“ he said quietly. „This is enough for the state attorney.“

„Not yet.“ I stepped closer, close enough to smell the cedar of his jacket and the faint bitter note of the champagne on his breath. „We need the board vote on record. We need them to endorse the financials knowing they're false. Otherwise they claim ignorance and this all becomes a paperwork error.“
He looked up at me. In the screen-light, his eyes were dark and searching. „You want to let them incriminate themselves.“
„I want to burn it so thoroughly that nothing grows back.“

The silence between us was its own kind of combustion. The conspiracy was the sex we weren't having — every document passed like a touch, every coded message like a confession, every midnight strategy session like a shared bed where we did nothing but talk. The charge was unbearable. The not-acting was the point. Anything physical would be simpler than this, and simple was the one thing we couldn't afford.
He turned back to the screen. I stood in the dark and felt the current slowly ebb from my fingertips.
Robert caught me in the upstairs corridor the next morning.

I was coming from the library — row 17-K, a fresh dispatch in *The Idea of a University* — and the timing was wrong, all wrong. My heart performed a short, ugly stutter against my ribs before I rammed it back into rhythm.
„Catherine.“ His voice was neutral. Morning voice. Boardroom voice. The voice he used when he was already three moves ahead in his head and waiting for you to catch up.
„Father.“ I slowed, adjusted my bag strap, composed my face into the mild pleasantness I'd been wearing for months. „You're in early.“
„You've been spending a lot of time near the chairman's office.“
Not a question. A statement, laid flat on the table like a card in a hand he hadn't finished revealing.
My pulse thudded once. I let it. Then I buried it.
„I've been trying to understand the new power structure.“ I met his eyes with the calm, practical gaze of a woman who'd been raised to read rooms. „Forewarned is forearmed. You taught me that.“
A beat of silence. Robert Vance studying his daughter the way he studied a contract — looking for the clause that would cost him later.
He nodded. „Good girl.“

The words should have been a relief. They weren't. Because his eyes lingered on my face for one second too long, and in that second I saw the thing I'd been afraid of since the beginning: my father was not stupid. He was many things — cruel, controlling, corrupted by decades of unexamined power — but he was not stupid, and he was beginning to notice that the landscape had shifted under his feet without an earthquake to explain it.

I walked away with my spine straight and my mind racing. The hallway stretched ahead of me, long and paneled and full of the particular hush that Blackwood wore like liturgy. I didn't look back. I didn't need to. I could feel his gaze on my shoulder blades, and I knew — with the cold animal certainty that had kept me alive in this family for twenty years — that the window was closing.
I told Heath that evening.

His office. The door locked. The cathedral lamp throwing amber light across the evidence board that dominated his east wall — the conspiracy map we'd been building for weeks, growing more detailed every day. Red thread connected photographs of board members to document summaries, dates to dollars, shell company names to the real people who'd profited. It was a web, intricate and expanding, and at the center of it all, a single date circled in red marker.
The charity gala. October 14th.
He listened to my account of the corridor confrontation without interrupting. When I finished, he stood and moved to the window, his reflection a dark outline against the autumn dusk.
„We accelerate,“ he said.
„He doesn't know anything yet. He's just—“

„He doesn't need to know anything. He needs to suspect. Robert's not the kind of man who waits for proof — he's the kind who acts on instinct and writes the justification later.“ Heath turned from the window. „Before he figures out we're working together, we need to have enough to make it irrelevant.“
The word *together* landed in the room and hummed.
„How much time?“ I asked.

„Weeks. Not months.“ He looked at the board, at the red circle on the calendar. „The gala. That's our deadline. The full board will be there. The important donors. The press. If we have the evidence by then, we don't need to go through channels — we can make it public in a venue they can't control.“
I looked at the board with him. The strings and pins and annotations. The faces of men who'd stolen from children's futures and called it stewardship. And that date, circled in red like a wound.
October 14th.
The charity gala.

The same event where, three years ago, everything had cracked — the night the pact fractured, the night Julian chose wrong, the night I learned that loyalty in this world was just a prettier word for leverage. I still had the memory filed in the part of my brain that never slept: the ballroom, the champagne, the look on Julian's face when he realized what his family had done and still chose them.
History was rhyming.
But this time we were writing the ending.
I turned to Heath. „The gala. Three weeks.“
He nodded. The lamplight caught the sharp edge of his jaw, the set of his shoulders, the stillness of a man who'd already committed to the leap and was just calculating the landing.
„Three weeks,“ he agreed.

Outside the window, the Blackwood grounds were going dark, the ancient oaks dissolving into the October sky. Somewhere in the main house, my father was making phone calls. Asking questions. Pulling threads of his own.
The clock was ticking.
And the gala was waiting.
